macro_segments.title: The Decoupling of Kofer from Skilah: Rabbi Yochanan's Position and Reish Lakish's Alternative

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Previous Discussion on Kofer and Skilah Coupling
Rav Dimi's Report of Rabbi Yochanan: The Extra Word 'Im' (If) Teaches That Kofer Is Independent of Skilah
Abaye's Challenge: If Kofer Is Decoupled from Skilah, Shouldn't the Thirty Shekels of the Eved Also Be Decoupled?
The Gemara's Response: Reish Lakish Disputes Rabbi Yochanan's Decoupling of Both Kofer and Eved Payment
Ravin's Report: Rabbi Yochanan Explicitly Teaches That Both Kofer and Eved Payment Are Decoupled from Skilah
The Gemara Explores Whether Reish Lakish Holds Both Linked or Both Decoupled: The Difference Between Textual Hermeneutics of Kofer and Eved
Resolution: The Extra Word 'Im' Appears Only in the Payment of Kofer, Not in the Payment of Eved, Allowing Different Interpretations
Summary of Three Positions: Coupled Both, Decoupled Both (Rabbi Yochanan), and Mixed Position (Reish Lakish on Eved)

macro_segments.title: The Goring of Children (Ben and Bat): Establishing Uniform Halachic Treatment Between Adults and Minors

micro_segments.title: Opening the Topic: The Parsha's Requirement to Teach That the Same Laws Apply When an Ox Gores a Child as When It Gores an Adult
Tanu Rabbanan: The Teaching That Liability for Goring Minors Is Identical to Liability for Goring Adults
The Logical Argument (Din Hu): Parallels to Murder (Adam B'Adam) as Support for Equal Treatment
The Refutation: Why Logic Alone Is Insufficient—Humans Face Four Additional Payments (Nezek, Tza'ar, Ripuy, Shevet, Boshet) That Oxen Do Not
The Solution: The Extra Pasuk 'O Ben Yigach O Bat Yigach' Is Necessary to Establish Uniform Treatment of Children and Adults
Learning from Muadim to Tam: Using the Principle That If Halacha Applies to Both Men and Women at the Tam Level, It Should Apply to Both at the Muad Level
The Kal Vachomer Argument: Children Have a Privileged Position in Damages Law (Never Liable), So Compensation Certainly Should Apply Equally
The Gemara's Objection to the Kal Vachomer: One Cannot Move from a Weightier Case (Muad, Intentional) to a Lighter Case (Tam, Unintentional)
The Alternative Argument: The Repeated Word 'Yigach' (Gores) in 'O Ben Yigach O Bat Yigach' Teaches Not to Distinguish Between Types of Gorings (Tam, Muad, Death, Injury)

macro_segments.title: Unintentional Goring (Shor Shelo B'Kavanah): Exemption from Skilah but Possible Payment of Kofer

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Cases of Exemption from Skilah: Rubbing Against Wall, Attempting to Kill an Animal Instead of a Person, or Killing a Non-Viable Fetus
The Gemara's Principle: Mitat HaBaal K'Mitat HaShor—Only When the Owner Would Be Executed for Murder Should the Ox Be Stoned
Shmuel's Position: Even in Cases Exempt from Skilah, Kofer Is Still Paid If the Ox Is a Muad
The Gemara's Investigation: Is the Ox in the Wall-Rubbing Case Really a Muad, and How Can It Avoid Skilah If It Knows People Are Present?

Resolution: The Ox Scratches Itself Against the Wall Incidentally, Not Intentionally Trying to Kill—After Falling on Someone, It Continues Scratching, Proving Its Motive Was Self-Scratching, Not Murder
The Problem of Tzerorot (Rolling Stones): Can Kofer Be Paid When Damage Is Caused by Indirect Means (Stones Rolling) Rather Than Direct Body Contact?
Rav Mari's Resolution: If the Ox's Body Continues Rubbing Against the Stone as It Rolls Onto the Person, Direct Body Contact (Gufo) Is Maintained, Not Mere Koach (Indirect Force)

macro_segments.title: Baraita Establishing Four Combinations: Skilah and Kofer Are Linked to Different Factors (Kavanah vs. Muad)

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's Framework: Some Cases Combine Skilah with Kofer, Some Have Only Kofer, Some Only Skilah, and Some Have Neither
Case 1 (Muad B'Kavanah): Deliberate Goring by a Habitual Ox Results in Both Skilah and Kofer
Case 2 (Muad Shelo B'Kavanah): Unintentional Harm by a Habitual Ox Results in Kofer Only (No Skilah)
Case 3 (Tam B'Kavanah): Deliberate Goring by an Ox on First Offense Results in Skilah Only (No Kofer)
Case 4 (Tam Shelo B'Kavanah): Unintentional Harm by an Ox on First Offense Results in Exemption from Both
Conclusion: Skilah Depends on Kavanah (Intent); Kofer Depends on Muad (Habituality)—Independent Factors

macro_segments.title: Damage (Nezikin) When Unintentional: The Debate Between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon on Payment for Harm to Wrong Party

micro_segments.title: The Question: If an Ox Intentionally Tries to Gore One Animal but Injures a Different Person, Who Pays What?
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Damages Are Paid Even When Unintentionally Targeting a Different Party, Drawn from the Analogy to Kofer
Rabbi Shimon's Position: Damages Are Not Paid If the Harm Was Not the Intended Target, Drawn from the Analogy to Skilah (Mitat HaBaal K'Mitat HaShor)
The Gemara's Explanation: Rabbi Yehuda Analogizes to Tashlumin (Payments), Not Mita (Death); Rabbi Shimon Focuses on the Ox's Responsibility
The Conclusion: The Mishna Reflects Rabbi Yehuda's View; Tried to Gore One Person and Gored Another Results in Liability (for a Human Perpetrator, This Would Be Murder)

macro_segments.title: Intentional Harm to Wrong Party (Tried to Kill One Person, Killed Another): The Dispute Between Rabbi Shimon and the Rabbanan on Criminal Liability

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case: Ox Is Stoned If It Tried to Gore One Person and Gored Another, Implying Liability Even When Intent and Outcome Mismatch
Rabbi Shimon's Dispute: Exemption If the Intended Victim Was Not Someone Deserving Death (Animal, Non-Viable Fetus, or Non-Jew)
Rabbi Shimon's Source: The Pasuk 'Ve'Arav Lo Vekam Alav' (He Laid in Wait for Him) Requires Specific Intent Toward the Actual Victim
Rabbanan's Rebuttal: The Pasuk's 'Laid in Wait' Only Excludes Cases Where No Specific Victim Was Intended (Throwing Stone Into a Crowd)
The Principle of Kavua (Fixed): When a Non-Jew Is Among a Group of Jews, the Act Cannot Be Characterized as Intentional Murder of a Jew if the Majority Were Non-Jews
The Pasuk's Addition of Kavua Doctrine: Even If the Majority of the Crowd Are Jews, the Presence of One Non-Jew Creates a Doubtful Scenario Treated as Fifty-Fifty (Safeik Nefashot L'Hakel)

macro_segments.title: Ownership Requirements for Skilah: Whether an Ox Must Have an Owner at the Time of the Goring or Throughout the Process

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Teaching: An Ox Owned by a Woman, Orphans, an Apotropos, or One Without an Owner (Wild Ox, Temple Property, Converted Proselyte's Estate) Is Still Subject to Skilah
Rabbi Yehuda's Dispute: A Wild Ox, Temple Ox, or an Ox Originally Owned by a Proselyte Who Died Without Heirs Cannot Be Stoned Because It Lacks an Owner
Rav Huna's Ruling: Rabbi Yehuda Would Even Exempt an Ox That Gored While Owned but Was Subsequently Dedicated to the Temple or Made Ownerless
The Distinction Between Wild Ox and Proselyte's Estate: The Mishna Lists Both to Teach That Ownership Must Be Maintained Throughout the Judgment Process, Not Just at the Time of the Goring
The Textual Source: The Pasuk 'VeHu'ad B'Va'alav' (It Is Warned to Its Owners) and 'Vehashor Yisakel' (The Ox Shall Be Stoned) Implies Both Ownership and Judgment Must Occur Simultaneously
Gemara's Challenge: Must the Judgment Be Completed (Gemar Din) for the Ox to Be Termed 'Shor Haniskal' (The Stoned Ox)?

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yochanan vs. Reish Lakish (on kofer/skilah coupling)
Ben and Bat (child victims in goring)
Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon (on unintended damages and wrong victim)
Rabbanan (sages) vs. Rabbi Shimon (on intent to kill wrong person)
Mitat HaBaal K'Mitat HaShor (owner's execution parallel to ox stoning)
Kavua (fixed/definite status in case of doubt)
Safeik Nefashot L'Hakel (doubt in capital cases leans toward leniency)
